questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: “System of Rice Intensification” of cultivation, in which alternate wetting and drying of rice fields is practiced, results in:
1. Reduced seed requirement
2. Reduced methane production
3. Reduced electricity consumption
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: “System of Rice Intensification” of cultivation, in which alternate wetting and drying of rice fields is practiced, results in: 1. Reduced seed requirement 2. Reduced methane production 3. Reduced electricity consumption Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **System of Rice Intensification (SRI) includes a method called Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) which is a form of controlled or intermittent irrigation of the rice crops. SRI methods have the following benefits and impacts, in general, compared to conventional methods of paddy cultivation: - To increase paddy yields usually by 20-50% and sometimes up to 100% -**It reduces required seeds for transplanting by 60-80% (Hence
Option 1 is correct); **-**The electricity consumption will be less as it enables **reduce use of chemical fertilizers and agrichemicals and reduced use of irrigation water by 25-50% (**Hence
Option 3 is correct)**;** Option 2 is correct. **By stopping flooding and relying mostly on organic fertilization,**methane emissions are greatly **reduced without offsetting increases in nitrous oxide**. Also,**less carbon dioxide **is generated due to reduced reliance on and transport of manufactured inputs. Oxford researchers in India found 30% reduction in net greenhouse gas emissions with SRI, compared with current practice, and more than 50% reduction in greenhouse gas emissions per kilogram of rice produced.

statementLines: With reference to the Pala dynasty, consider the following statements about King Dharmapala of the Pala dynasty:
I. He established the ancient Vikramshila University.
II. He patronised Haribhadra, a Buddhist philosopher.
III. The Khalimpur Copper Plate Inscription was issued by him.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Pala dynasty, consider the following statements about King Dharmapala of the Pala dynasty: I. He established the ancient Vikramshila University. II. He patronised Haribhadra, a Buddhist philosopher. III. The Khalimpur Copper Plate Inscription was issued by him. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Dharmapala (770–815 CE) **succeeded his father Gopala I and made the Pala kingdom a force to reckon with. Bengal and Bihar were directly ruled by him.**

Statement I is correct: Vikramshila University is an ancient Buddhist learning center established by King **Dharampala of the Pala Dynasty.**It was one of the most prestigious educational institutions of its time, comparable to Nalanda University.**

Statement II is correct: **Dharmapala is known for his patronage of Buddhism, apart from his military campaigns. He founded many Buddhist monasteries for the promotion of Buddhist learning. He**patronised Haribhadra, a Buddhist **philosopher. **

Statement III is correct: Khalimpur Copper Plate Inscription was issued by Dharmapala** (775-810 CE) in the 32nd regnal year after his victory at Kannauj.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The ‘Dying Princess’ painting is primarily associated with which among the following caves?
questions.questionText: The ‘Dying Princess’ painting is primarily associated with which among the following caves?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Option a is correct:**The**‘Dying Princess’ painting **belonged to the period of 5th century CE and** is located in Cave **No. 16 of Ajanta Caves in Maharashtra. **It is created using the** tempera technique. **The painting**represents**the last moments of Sundari, the**heartbroken wife of Buddha’s brother, Nanda, who left her **to become a monk. **Emotions are expressed here by the controlled turn and poise of the body, and the eloquent gestures of hands and eyes. The dying princess is reclining against a bolster, and a female attendant is holding her. The bent head of the princess, her lowered eyes, the drooping limbs and the sad, tense female attendants; all hint at imminent death. Though deserted by her husband, in her last moments, the dying princess is surrounded by deeply concerned attendants

statementLines: Which of the following books were written by Raja Bhoja of Paramaras?
I. Kavirajamarga
II. Shringara Prakash
III. Champu Ramayan
IV. Samarangan-Sutradhara
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following books were written by Raja Bhoja of Paramaras? I. Kavirajamarga II. Shringara Prakash III. Champu Ramayan IV. Samarangan-Sutradhara Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Bhoja (c.1010−1055 CE) is the most celebrated ruler of the Paramara dynasty,**and under him the dynasty reached its zenith. Under his reign, the Paramaras extended their kingdom from Chittor in the north to upper Konkan in the south, and from the Sabarmati River in the west to Vidisha in the east.** Option I is incorrect: Amoghavarsha I (814–880 CE)**of the Rashtrakuta dynasty was himself a great poet and**wrote **Kavirajamarga (and not Raja Bhoja of the Paramara dynasty)**, the earliest Kannada work on poetics.** Option II is correct: Shringara Prakash was written by Raja Bhoja**of the Paramara dynasty. It has 36 chapters (Preakasha). In the first eight chapters there is a representation of the ideas of semantic grammar. The Gunas in the ninth, the defects in the tenth, the epic in the eleventh, and the drama in the twelfth.** Option III is correct: **A**Sanskrit Ramayana version in Champu**(poetry and prose mixed)**form was primarily written **by King Bhoja,**with later parts completed by Lakshmana Suri and Venkatraj. Bhojaraja composed the work up to Kishkinda kanda and Lakshman Bhatt completed the work up to Yudhakanda. Venkata raju completed the work by adding Uttara Kanda.  Option IV is correct: Samarangan-Sutradhara was written by Paramara King Bhoja. **In this text, the qualifications of a sthapati are described in great length, and it**underlined the disadvantages of selecting the wrong man for the **construction of temples and other structures.**It is stated in another part of the same book that sthapati ought to be worshipped in order to appease Tvashtri, the divine architect and son of Lord Vishwakarma.

statementLines: In the context of early medieval India, Vijnaneshwara and Jimutavahana are best known for:
questions.questionText: In the context of early medieval India, Vijnaneshwara and Jimutavahana are best known for:

options.a: Compilation of medical treatises that systematised surgical practices of the time.
options.b: Establishment of a new school of temple architecture under the later Chalukyas of Kalyani.
options.c: Composition of devotional hymns that laid the foundation of the Bhakti movement in South India.
options.d: Contributions to the legal treatises that defined inheritance laws.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Option d is correct:**In early medieval India, a large number of important and influential Dharmasastra compilations, digests and commentaries came to be written. This points to the processes of formalisation of law and legal procedures which helped the state to regulate and arbitrate in the social life of its subjects. Vijnaneshwara and Jimutavahana are best known for their**contributions to legal treatises that defined inheritance laws. **Vijnaneshwara**(11-12th century), along with Apararka (12th century),**wrote commentaries on the Yajnavalkya Smriti. **Vijnaneshvara’s commentary, Mitakshara, **became an authority on various aspects of Hindu law.**Jimutavahana wrote a work **on procedural law called the Vyavaharamatrika and**a digest of laws on inheritance **called the Dayabhaga.**

statementLines: In medieval India, the term ‘Vativaykkal’ refers to:
questions.questionText: In medieval India, the term ‘Vativaykkal’ refers to:

options.a: A traditional way of harnessing rainwater in the Kavery (Cauvery) delta.
options.b: A method of land assessment for the collection of land revenue in the Southern parts of India.
options.c: A memorial stone erected to commemorate the honorable deaths of heroes in the battle.
options.d: A guild of maritime merchants engaged in overseas trad

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **During the medieval Indian period, the**Cholas kingdom of South India**undertook significant measures to improve the irrigation system that was already in practice. As the state was drawing most of its revenue from agriculture, the Cholas focused their efforts on managing water resources efficiently.**Vativaykkal, a criss-cross channel, is a traditional way of harnessing rain water in the Kavery (Cauvery) delta**. In this system, vati runs in the north–south direction, while**vaykkal** runs in the east–west direction. Technically, vati is a drainage channel and vaykkal is a supply channel.
The water running through vaykkal to the field was to be drained out to vati and then to another vaykkal. Rain water would flow from where the natural canal started, thereby ensuring systematic distribution and drainage of water across the fields.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **After the decline of the Cholas, the Pandya kingdom became the leading Tamil dynasty in the thirteenth century, with Madurai as its capital.**The Pandyan kingdom was also known for their pearl fisheries. Marco Polo – an Italian **explorer – speaks of the extremely fine quality of the pearls from this kingdom. Their major port cities were: **Korkai, Kayal, Periyapattinam etc. ** The Pandyas had intercontinental trade. The foreign merchants and travellers addressed the Pandyan kingdom as Mabar which is an Arabic term for ferry or passage.
Knowledge Base: Marco Polo visited Kayal twice, in 1288 and in 1293. He tells us that this port town was full of ships from Arabia and China and bustling with business activities. In Kayal, there was an agency established by an Arab chieftain by name Maliku-l-Islam Jamaluddin, and this agency facilitated the availability of horses to Pandya kings.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Delhi Sultanate, consider the following statements about Iltutmish:
I. The Sultanate, for the first time, had to face a Mongol threat of invasion of India during his reign.
II. He shifted the capital of the sultanate from Lahore to Delhi.
III. He established Turkan-i-Chahalgani (Group of forty), who advised and helped him in administering the Sultanate.
IV. He introduced the ceremony of sijada and paibos in the King’s court.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Delhi Sultanate, consider the following statements about Iltutmish: I. The Sultanate, for the first time, had to face a Mongol threat of invasion of India during his reign. II. He shifted the capital of the sultanate from Lahore to Delhi. III. He established Turkan-i-Chahalgani (Group of forty), who advised and helped him in administering the Sultanate. IV. He introduced the ceremony of sijada and paibos in the King’s court. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Iltutmish (1211–1236 CE) belonged to the Ilbari tribe and was originally a slave who was later purchased by Qutb-ud-din Aibak. Recognizing his ability and loyalty, Aibak appointed him as the iqtadar of Gwalior. After Aibak’s death, Iltutmish defeated Aram Shah (Aram Baksh) in 1211 CE and ascended the throne of Delhi.**

Statement I is correct:**The first Mongol threat to India began as early as 1221 CE during the reign of Iltutmish **when Temujin, popularly known as Genghis Khan, launched campaigns across Central Asia after destroying the Khwarizmi Empire. Chenghiz Khan reached the Indian frontiers in pursuit of the crown-prince Jalaluddin Mangbarni. Jalaluddin sought refuge from Sultan Iltutmish of the Delhi Sultanate. He diplomatically saved India by refusing to support the Khwarizmi Shah Jalaluddin of Central Asia against the Mongol ruler Chengiz Khan.**

Statement II is correct:**During the reign of Qutb-ud-din Aibak, he established his capital in Lahore to counter the ambitions of Taj al-Din Yildiz (Yalduz), who sought to annex Punjab. However, his successor**Iltutmish later **transferred the capital back from Lahore to Delhi. **

Statement III is correct: Iltutmish established the ‘Group of Forty’ (Turkan-i-Chahalgani). **These were Turkish amirs (nobles) who advised and helped the Sultan in administering the Sultanate. For a few years they decided on the selection of Sultans one after the other. The group was finally eliminated by Balban.**

Statement IV is incorrect:**The ceremony of sijada (prostration) and paibos (kissing of the monarch’s feet) in the **king’s court was introduced by Balban**(AD 1266–87), not by Iltutmish.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: With reference to major crops in India, consider the following information:
Crop Crop Type (Primarily) Largest Producer State (FY 2024-25)
I. Sugarcane Kharif crop Uttar Pradesh
II. Jowar Kharif crop Maharashtra
III. Tur (Arhar) Rabi crop Jharkhand
IV. Maize Rabi crop Karnataka In
how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.questionText: With reference to major crops in India, consider the following information: Crop Crop Type (Primarily) Largest Producer State (FY 2024-25) I. Sugarcane Kharif crop Uttar Pradesh II. Jowar Kharif crop Maharashtra III. Tur (Arhar) Rabi crop Jharkhand IV. Maize Rabi crop Karnataka In how many of the above rows is the information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**Row I is correct**.**Sugarcane**is a tropical crop that requires a long growing season, plenty of water and warm temperatures. In most parts of India, it is planted with the onset of the monsoon. Therefore, it is a**Kharif crops**, although its growing period extends much longer than typical seasonal crops.**Uttar Pradesh**has fertile alluvial soils and extensive canal irrigation in the Ganga–Yamuna Doab region. These factors make it the**largest producer of **sugarcane in India**. In FY**2024-25**, Uttar Pradesh accounted for 48.57% of the total Sugarcane production, Maharashtra 24.19% of total production and Karnataka 10.57% of total production.**Row II is correct**.**Jowar (sorghum**) is mainly grown as a**Kharif crop**, though it is also grown in Rabi season in some regions. Also, the government while announcing MSP treats it as a Kharif crop and not rabbi crop.**Maharashtra**is a leading producer of Jowar due to its semi-arid climate and black soil regions. In FY**2024-25**, Maharashtra accounted for 37.24% of the total Jowar production, Karnataka-16.45% of total production and Rajasthan 10.50% of total production.**Row III is incorrect**.**Tur **is also known as arhar. It is the second most important pulse crop after the gram** and a **major Kharif crop in India. Maharashtra is the largest producer of Tur (arhar). **In FY**2024-25**, Maharashtra accounted for 39.24% of the total tur production, Karnataka-27.52% of total production and Jharkhand 7.03% of total production.**Row IV is incorrect**.**Maize**is a versatile cereal crop. It is primarily cultivated during the**Kharif season**, though it can also be grown in other seasons under irrigation.**Karnataka**, though an important maize-producing state, is not the largest producer but the 2nd largest producer of Maize in India. In FY**2024-25**, Madhya Pradesh accounted for 15.3% of the total maize production, Karnataka accounted for 14.26% and Bihar accounted for 12.60%.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The famous female figurine known as ‘Dancing Girl’, found at Mohenjo-daro, is made of:
questions.questionText: The famous female figurine known as ‘Dancing Girl’, found at Mohenjo-daro, is made of:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The “Dancing Girl” figurine, a famous artifact from the Indus Valley Civilization, was discovered in 1926 by archaeologist Ernest Mackay at Mohenjo-daro. This**bronze **statuette, depicting a nude young woman or girl, is 10.5 cm tall and is notable for its confident, naturalistic pose. It depicts a young girl in a pose suggestive of dance, with one hand on her hip and a confident stance, showcasing both artistic sensibility and casting skill. The**material **used to make the figurine is**bronze**, and it was created using the**lost-wax casting technique**, which was highly advanced for that era. This artifact is considered a**masterpiece of Harappan craftsmanship **and a symbol of the cultural sophistication of the Indus Valley people.

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which of the following statements with reference to Makhana are correct?
I. It is ideally cultivated in stagnant perennial water bodies where a water depth of 1-1.5 meters is maintained throughout the year.
II. India is the largest producer of makhana in the world.
III. The National Makhana Board in India is established in the state of Bihar.
IV. A central sector scheme by the Government of India for the development of Makhana has been launched recently.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with reference to Makhana are correct? I. It is ideally cultivated in stagnant perennial water bodies where a water depth of 1-1.5 meters is maintained throughout the year. II. India is the largest producer of makhana in the world. III. The National Makhana Board in India is established in the state of Bihar. IV. A central sector scheme by the Government of India for the development of Makhana has been launched recently. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**.**Makhana**, also known as**Gorgon**nut**or**foxnut**, is an aquatic crop. It is also known as “**Black Diamond**” as Makhana plant consists of small, round seeds that have an outer layer ranging from black to brown. It is**highly nutritious**and provides an excellent source of**carbohydrates**,**protein**, and**minerals**.**

Statement I is correct**.**Makhana is an aquatic crop. Makhana is ideally cultivated in stagnant perennial water **bodies like ponds, land depressions, oxbow lakes, swamps and ditches where a water depth of 1-1.5 meter is **maintained throughout the year. **It grows well in humid to sub-humid conditions and at an air temperature of 20°C-35°C, relative humidity of 50-90% and annual rainfall of 100-250 cm is conducive for its optimum growth.**

Statement II is correct**.**India is the largest producer of makhana in the world**, accounting for nearly 80 per cent of global production. Bihar is the backbone of the country’s makhana output, contributing around 85 per cent of national production, with Darbhanga emerging as one of the key hubs for cultivation and processing.**

Statement III is correct**. The establishment of National Makhana Board in Bihar was announced in Union Budget**2025-26**with primary objectives to improve production, processing, value addition and marketing.**The Union **Government has established the National Makhana Board in Bihar.**

Statement IV is correct**. The Government of India has approved a Central Sector Scheme for Development of Makhana with an outlay of ₹476.03 crore for the period**2025-26**to**2030-31**. The scheme focuses on research and innovation, quality seed production, farmer capacity building, improved harvesting and post-harvest practices, value addition, branding and marketing, export promotion, and quality control.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**The**victory at Talikota also led to the commencement of a golden period for Bijapur,**which saw a flurry of construction as Hindu artisans from the defeated capital of Vijayanagara sought new patrons. Bijapur entered its moment of glory with the ascension of Ibrahim Adil Shah II in 1580, who ruled for almost 50 years and is considered the greatest Adil Shahi Sultan.**Ibrahim Adil Shah II (**1580-16**27) was addressed as “Jagat Guru”, for, as an aesthete steeped in musical and literary **pursuits**, he was a devotee of Goddess Saraswati. In some of the farmans (royal edicts) issued during his rule, he is**referred to as Az Puja Shri Saraswati**.**Ibrahim Adil Shah II’s own composition, Kitab-e-Nauras, contains lavish **praise of Goddess Saraswati**. Ibrahim Adil Shah II was also more comfortable in Marathi, a consequence of the ruling dynasty’s close links with the Maratha nobility and the status that the language had as the lingua franca, than with Persian, which he spoke with some difficulty.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In the context of Indian Art & Culture, the towns of Nirmal, Etikoppaka, and Kondapalli are traditionally famous for which of the following?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian Art & Culture, the towns of Nirmal, Etikoppaka, and Kondapalli are traditionally famous for which of the following?

options.a: Painting of hand-drawn Kalamkari textile fabrics.
options.b: Manufacture of glazed blue pottery and ceramic artefacts.
options.c: Production of indigenous wooden toys and handicrafts.
options.d: Casting of bronze sculptures in the Chola styl

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**towns of Nirmal (Telangana), Etikoppaka (Andhra Pradesh), and Kondapalli (Andhra Pradesh)**are traditionally**renowned for their centuries-old tradition of crafting wooden toys**. These are handmade, eco-friendly handicrafts made from soft/lightweight woods like Tella Poniki (in Kondapalli) or similar local varieties, and finished with natural lacquer, vibrant natural dyes, or detailed paintings.**I. Nirmal**is famous for its**painted wooden toys**and decorative articles. The craft tradition includes mythological figures, animals, and rural scenes. I**t was awarded the Geographical Indication (GI) tag in 2009.**II. Etikoppaka**is known for its lacquered wooden toys made from Ankudu wood, coloured traditionally with natural dyes through a**turning-and-lacquering technique. The GI tag provided to these toys in 2017. **III. Kondapalli**is known for Kondapalli Bommalu, lightweight wooden toys carved from softwood (Tella Poniki). They often depict themes from mythology, rural life, and animals (like the famous "**Ambari" elephants**). It was granted with**GI tag in 2006**.

It exemplifies the architectural sophistication of the Vijayanagara Empire and was constructed in the 15th century during the reign of Devaraya II. questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The**temple best described in the passage is Vitthala Temple**(also known as Shri Vijaya Vittala Temple or Vittala Temple) located in**Hampi**,**Karnataka**. - It is**famous for its ‘musical pillars’**(also called Saregama pillars) in the Ranga Mandapa, which produce different musical notes when gently tapped. - The temple was constructed in the**15th century during the reign of Devaraya II (r. 1422–1446 CE)**of the Vijayanagara Empire. - The temple is**built in**the**Dravidian architectural style**, characterized by a large courtyard, beautifully carved pillars, and intricate sculptures depicting various deities. - The Temple is dedicated to**Lord Vitthala**, an**incarnation of Lord Vishnu.**- It is located in the north eastern part of Hampi, near the**banks of the Tungabhadra River. **

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to medieval Indian military tactics, the term ‘Tulghuma/Tulughma system’ refers to:
questions.questionText: With reference to medieval Indian military tactics, the term ‘Tulghuma/Tulughma system’ refers to:

options.a: A method of launching surprise night attacks on enemy encampments.
options.b: A method of dividing the whole army into small units and placing them on the flanks.
options.c: A system of maintaining branded horses and descriptive rolls of soldiers.
options.d: A practice of recruiting soldiers through local chieftains and tribal leaders.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**Tulghuma/Tulughma system**was a**military tactic introduced in India by Babur**, the founder of the Mughal Empire. It was first effectively used during the**First Battle of Panipat (1526)**against the numerically superior forces of Ibrahim Lodi. Under this system, the**whole army**was**divided into several small, highly mobile units**, especially cavalry, which were**positioned on the flanks**. These flanking units would move swiftly to**encircle the enemy from the sides and **rear**, creating confusion and breaking enemy formations. Knowledge Base:**Araba: **It referred to the**use of carts (araba) placed in large numbers in rows**facing the enemy and tied to each other with ropes, forming a defensive barricade.**Babur**effectively used the**Araba**system along with the**Tulghuma tactic**in the**First Battle of Panipat** (1526).

statementLines: Consider the following statements:

Who is being described in the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: I. In 1610, after expelling the Mughals from Ahmednagar, he established a new capital, a city named Khirki (present- day Aurangabad in Maharashtra) for the sultanate. II. Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, in his grand epic poem ‘Sivabharata’, also mentioned him, referring to him “as brave as the sun”. Who is being described in the above statements?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Malik Ambar was an African slave turned warrior. The resurgence of the Ahmadnagar (Nizam Shahi) Sultanate (Kingdom) against Mughal expansion was primarily due to the efforts of Malik Ambar.**In 1610, after expelling the **Mughals from Ahmednagar, Ambar established a new capital, a city named Khirki (present-day Aurangabad in **Maharashtra) for the sultanate. **He is credited with establishing a more efficient land revenue model of the time which was used by the Marathas under Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj, whose grandfather (Maloji) was a close aide of Ambar.** In years to come, **Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj**in his grand epic poem ‘Sivabharata’ also mentioned Ambar, referring him “as brave as **the sun”. **

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Maratha polity in the 18th century, the Sangola Agreement is significant because:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Maratha polity in the 18th century, the Sangola Agreement is significant because:

options.a: It provided the Peshwa with more authority, and the king (Chhatrapati) was reduced to a titular king.
options.b: It established a subsidiary alliance between the Marathas and the British East India Company.
options.c: It marked the formal recognition of the 'Chauth' and 'Sardeshmukhi' rights over the Mughal Deccan provinces.
options.d: It divided the Maratha Empire into autonomous confederacies under regional chiefs.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Sangola Agreement (1750) was a crucial turning point in the political history of the Maratha polity. It was concluded between the Chhatrapati Rajaram II and the Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao (Nanasaheb).**In 1750, Ramraja signed the famous Sangola agreement, whereby he invested the Peshwa with more authority and **reduced himself to a titular king. **This was in return for continuing his rights as a Chhatrapati and protection from Tarabai and her henchmen.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: In Medieval India, the designations ‘Mahattara’ and ‘Pattakila’ were used for
(a) military officers
(b) village headmen
(c) specialists in Vedic rituals
(d) chiefs of craft guilds
questions.questionText: In Medieval India, the designations ‘Mahattara’ and ‘Pattakila’ were used for
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Mahattara refers to “representatives of towns or villages” **and was a title used in the administration during the rule of the Silahara dynasty (765-1215 AD) Pattakila also refers to village headman.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the reign of Firoz Shah Tughlaq, consider the following statements:
I. The holders of iqta acquired hereditary rights during his reign.
II. During his reign, a separate department of slaves (diwan-i bandagan) was set up.
III. He introduced payment of salaries in cash to soldiers.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the reign of Firoz Shah Tughlaq, consider the following statements: I. The holders of iqta acquired hereditary rights during his reign. II. During his reign, a separate department of slaves (diwan-i bandagan) was set up. III. He introduced payment of salaries in cash to soldiers. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Firoz Shah Tughlaq (r. 1351–1388 CE)**was one of the prominent rulers of the**Delhi Sultanate**and belonged to the**Tughlaq dynasty**. He**succeeded Muhammad bin Tughlaq**(r. **1325-13**51)**at a time when the Sultanate was politically unstable and financially strained.**

Statement I is correct:**Firoz Shah Tughlaq revived**and strengthened the**iqta system**and, unlike some of his predecessors, he**also made the system hereditary**. He pleased the nobles and assured hereditary succession to their properties.**

Statement II is correct**:**Firoz Shah Tughlaq**maintained a large number of slaves (bandagan) and**created a separate **department called Diwan-i-Bandagan**to manage their welfare, training, and administration.**

Statement III is incorrect: Alauddin Khalji (1296–1316) introduced the payment of salaries in cash to soldiers**, which led to price regulations popularly called Market Reforms.**Feroz Tughluq gave up the practice of paying his royal soldiers in cash**: instead, he gave them a paper called itlaq-a sort of draft on whose strength they could claim their salary from the Sultan's revenue officers of the khalisa ("Crown" or "reserve" land).

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the temples of India, which one among the following was made earliest?
questions.questionText: With reference to the temples of India, which one among the following was made earliest?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**

Option a is incorrect**.**Chennakeshava Temple, Belur,**was consecrated by the famous Hoysala king Vishnuvardhana to mark his victories in**1116 CE**against the Cholas and called the Vijaya Narayana.**

Option b is correct**. The**Lakshmana Temple**was built in the**10th century CE**by the Chandella ruler Yashovarman. It is one of the earliest surviving temples in the Khajuraho group and an early example of fully developed Nagara architecture in central India. Yasovarman (954 CE) built the temple of Vishnu, now famous as Lakshmana temple is an ornate and evolved example of its time, proclaiming the prestige of the Chandellas.**

Option c is incorrect**. The**Sun Temple at Modhera**in Gujarat was constructed around 1026-27** CE**(**early 11th century **CE**) during the reign of Solanki ruler Bhima I. It is famous for its grand stepped tank and ornate mandapa.**

Option d is incorrect**. The**Konark Sun Temple**was built in the**13th century CE** by the Eastern Ganga ruler Narasimhadeva I.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to pilgrimage sites in India, consider the following:
I. Sittanavasal Caves
II. Shatrunjaya Hills
III. Dilwara Temples
How many of the above are recognised as important Jain pilgrimage sites?
questions.questionText: With reference to pilgrimage sites in India, consider the following: I. Sittanavasal Caves II. Shatrunjaya Hills III. Dilwara Temples How many of the above are recognised as important Jain pilgrimage sites?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. Jainism has a long architectural and pilgrimage tradition across different regions of India. Many rock-cut caves, hill shrines and temple complexes are associated with Jain monks, Tirthankaras and religious practices.** Option I is correct**.**Sittanavasal Caves are located in Tamil Nadu**. They are rock-cut caves associated with Jain monks and are known for their early medieval Jain mural paintings.**The site functioned as a Jain monastic centre**and is regarded as an important Jain heritage location.** Option II is correct**.**Shatrunjaya Hills, located in Gujarat near Palitana**, are among the most sacred pilgrimage centres of Jainism. The hills are covered with numerous Jain temples dedicated to different Tirthankaras. This sacred site contains hundreds of shrines that were sanctified when**Bhagwan Rishabhadev, the first Tirthankara of Jainism**,**gave his first sermon**in the temple on the hilltop.** Option III is correct**. The**Dilwara Temples, located at Mount Abu in Rajasthan**, are renowned Jain temples built between the 11th and 13th centuries. They are dedicated to different Jain Tirthankaras and are celebrated for their intricate marble carvings.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following pairs of temples and the dynasties under which they were built:
Temple Built during the rule of
I. Sri Veerabhadra Temple, Laapakshi Vijayanagara Dynasty
II. Pancha Rathas, Mahabalipuram Pallava Dynasty
III. Pattadakal Group of Temples, Pattadakal Hoysala Dynasty
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Consider the following pairs of temples and the dynasties under which they were built: Temple Built during the rule of I. Sri Veerabhadra Temple, Laapakshi Vijayanagara Dynasty II. Pancha Rathas, Mahabalipuram Pallava Dynasty III. Pattadakal Group of Temples, Pattadakal Hoysala Dynasty How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**Pair I is correct**. Sri Veerabhadra temple, also known as Lepakshi Temple is located in Lepakshi Village, named after a main shrine dedicated to Veerabhadra (the fiery incarnation of Lord Shiva).**The Veerabhadra temple at Lepakshi was **constructed during King Achutaraya’s reign in 1530 CE (Vijayanagara Dynasty). **This complex was part of the Vijayanagara Empire and embodies the architectural elements of that period which include an abundance of sculptures and paintings that depict stories from the Ramayana and Mahabharata.**Pair II is correct**.**The Pancha Rathas at Mahabalipuram were built during the reign of the Pallava ruler **Narasimhavarman I in the 7th-8th century CE**. These are monolithic rock-cut structures and represent early Dravidian architectural experimentation under the Pallavas.**Pair III is incorrect**.**The Pattadakal Group of Temples in Karnataka was built by the Early Chalukyas**. These temples are significant for combining both Nagara and Dravida architectural styles, reflecting experimentation during the Chalukya period. The**oldest temple at Pattadakal is Sangamesvara built by Vijayaditya Satyasraya (AD 697-733)**. The other notable temples at Pattadakal are the**Kadasiddhesvara, Jambulingeswara**both attributed to 7th century A.D.

statementLines: Consider the following statements with reference to Lalitaditya Muktapida of Kashmir:
I. The Rajatarangini, written by Kalhana, documents the rule of Lalitaditya Muktapida.
II. The Martand Sun Temple in Jammu and Kashmir’s Anantnag was built during his reign.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with reference to Lalitaditya Muktapida of Kashmir: I. The Rajatarangini, written by Kalhana, documents the rule of Lalitaditya Muktapida. II. The Martand Sun Temple in Jammu and Kashmir’s Anantnag was built during his reign. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Lalitaditya Muktapida **was one of the most illustrious kings**of the Karkota dynasty. **He was able to extend the power of Kashmir beyond the normal mountain limits. Lalitaditya was able to vanquish the Turks, Tibetans, Bhutias, Kambojas, and others.**

Statement I is correct: According to the Rajatarangini written by Kalhana, Lalitaditya Muktapida (8th century CE) **undertook extensive conquests and raids, reaching eastern India**on the one hand and parts of Central and Western Asia (Sinkiang and Iran) on the other. His main success was against King Yasovarman of Kannauj. After ascending the throne following Tarapida, he expanded his dominions to include Punjab, Kannauj, Tibet, Badakhshan, and other nearby territories.**

Statement II is correct: Lalitaditya Muktapida built the famous Martand Sun Temple in the Anantnag district of **Jammu and Kashmir,**which is regarded as**one of the earliest** and most magnificent surviving Sun temples in India and one of the largest temple complexes of that time.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
statementLines: With reference to the Rashtrakuta empire of Manyakheta or Malkhed, consider the following statements:
I. Dantidurga is considered the founder of the empire.
II. Al-Masudi visited India during the rule of the Rashtrakutas.
III. The Kailasa temple at Ellora was constructed during the reign of Rashtrakuta king Amoghavarsha I.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Rashtrakuta empire of Manyakheta or Malkhed, consider the following statements: I. Dantidurga is considered the founder of the empire. II. Al-Masudi visited India during the rule of the Rashtrakutas. III. The Kailasa temple at Ellora was constructed during the reign of Rashtrakuta king Amoghavarsha I. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **India witnessed the emergence of three powerful kingdoms between c. 750 and 1000 CE: Palas, Pratiharas and the Rashtrakutas. The**Rashtrakuta Empire dominated the Deccan for almost 200 years **and also controlled territories in north and south India at various points of time.**

Statement I is correct: Dantidurga was the founder of the imperial Rashtrakuta empire. **The capital of the Rastrakutas was Manyakheta or Malkhed.**

Statement II is correct: **According to**Al-Masudi **who**visited India at that time, the Rashtrakuta king Balhara or **Vallabharaja **was the greatest king of India and most of the Indian rulers accepted his suzerainty and respected his envoys.**

Statement III is incorrect: Rashtrakuta king Krishna I **built the famous** Kailasha temple at Ellora (near Aurangabad, **Maharashtra). **It is dedicated to Lord Shiva and is monolithic i.e. made of one single piece of rock.
Amoghavarsha I, the Rashtrakuta king, is best known as the author of the Kavirajamarga: the earliest work on poetics in Kannada.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: With reference to the Ellora Caves, Maharashtra, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Most of these caves were constructed during the Gupta period.
II. These caves were built as significant religious sites for Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism.
III. These caves are mentioned in the writings of the 10th century Arab geographer Al-Masudi.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Ellora Caves, Maharashtra, which of the following statements is/are correct? I. Most of these caves were constructed during the Gupta period. II. These caves were built as significant religious sites for Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. III. These caves are mentioned in the writings of the 10th century Arab geographer Al-Masudi. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.** Option I is incorrect: Ellora Caves date from the 6th to the 10th century CE. These were not constructed during the **Gupta period**. These caves could have received royal patronage from various dynasties, even though inscriptional evidences are lacking for most of them. The only definite inscriptional evidence is that of Rashtrakuta Dantidurga on the back wall of the front mandapa of Cave 15. The Great Kailasa (Cave 16) is attributed to Krishna I, the successor and uncle of Dantidurga. The**Ajanta Caves**were built in two phases. The first phase began around the 2nd century BCE during the rule of the Satavahana dynasty. The second phase, w**hich saw the creation of most of the caves, occurred during the Gupta **period**around the 5th to 6th century CE.**

Statement II is correct**. Ellora Caves are located in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra. The Ellora Caves are a remarkable example of India’s cultural and religious diversity,**showcasing Hindu, Buddhist, and Jain architecture in **a single site. Thus, these caves were built as significant religious sites for Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism. **

Statement II is correct**. The**Ellora caves, unlike Ajanta, have a distinction that they were never lost to oblivion**, due to their close proximity to the trade route. There have been numerous written records to indicate that Ellora caves were visited regularly by travellers and royal personages as well. The earliest is that of an**Arab geographer Al-Mas‘udi **(Masudi) of the 10th century CE. **

questions.subject: History
statementLines: Consider the following pairs with reference to different types of paintings in India:
Painting Primarily Associated with the State of
I. Madhubani Bihar
II. Pithoro Karnataka
III. Pichwai Rajasthan
IV. Warli Maharashtra
Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Consider the following pairs with reference to different types of paintings in India: Painting Primarily Associated with the State of I. Madhubani Bihar II. Pithoro Karnataka III. Pichwai Rajasthan IV. Warli Maharashtra Which of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Pair I is correct: Madhubani paintings are **traditionally done by the women of villages around**Madhubani town **(Bihar), **where it is also called Mithila Paintings. The paintings are usually drawn from religious motifs of the Hindus, including Krishna, Rama, Durga, Lakshmi and Shiva. The figures in the painting are symbolic, for example, fish depicts good luck and fertility. Some of the common features of these paintings include double line border, bold use of colours, ornate floral patterns and exaggerated facial features. It has been**given Geographical Indication (GI) status. **Pair II is incorrect: Pithora paintings are done by Bhil tribals of Gujarat and Madhya Pradesh (and not**Karnataka) **and are said to serve religious and spiritual purposes. They are painted in the walls of the houses to bring peace and prosperity. They are drawn on special family occasions as a ritual.**Depictions of animals are common, especially **horses. **Pair III is correct: Pichwai painting is a traditional Indian art form originating from Nathdwara in Rajasthan. **It is primarily associated with the worship of Lord Krishna, particularly in his manifestation as Shrinathji. These intricate paintings are usually done on cloth and are used as temple hangings. It depicts**scenes featuring Krishna,**often surrounded by gopis, cows, lotuses, and other symbols of his divine play (leelas). The use of natural colours, derived from minerals and vegetables, adds to their distinctiveness. Nathdwara is a prominent pilgrimage site for followers of the Pushtimarg sect of Hinduism.**Pair IV is correct: Warli paintings **are made by the Warli tribe, indigenous people that occupy mainly the**Gujarat
- **Maharashtra **border, who have been carrying the painting tradition that goes back to**2500-30**00 BC, These paintings have close resemblance to the mural paintings of Bhimbetka in Madhya Pradesh.They are made in geometrical shapes, either circle triangle or square. The circles represent sun or moon, triangles are resemblances of mountains or trees, and squares represent lands, or called ‘chauk’ or ‘chaukat’.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Performing Arts (Dance, Drama, Music, Martial Arts)
statementLines: Consider the following statements with reference to the Manipuri Classical dance:
I. The most unique aspect of the dance is that the dancers, throughout their performance, establish direct eye contact with the spectators.
II. Nagabandha mudra is an important posture in the dance.
III. Mangalacharan and Tharijham are important elements of the dance.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with reference to the Manipuri Classical dance: I. The most unique aspect of the dance is that the dancers, throughout their performance, establish direct eye contact with the spectators. II. Nagabandha mudra is an important posture in the dance. III. Mangalacharan and Tharijham are important elements of the dance. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Manipuri dance form finds its mythological origin in the celestial dance of Shiva and Parvati in the valleys of Manipur along with the local ‘Gandharvas’. The dance gained prominence with the advent of Vaishnavism in the 15th century.**Krishna became the central theme of this dance form.**It is generally performed by females.**

Statement I is incorrect:**The unique aspect of the dance is the restrained gestures and expressions of the dancers.**Throughout the performance, the dancers do not establish eye contact with the spectators.**Manipuri dance is unique due to its emphasis on devotion and not sensuality. The faces are covered with a thin veil and facial expression is of lesser importance, rather hand gestures and gentle movement of feet are important. It is characterised by its fluid movements where each movement appears to flow into the next one. It also has extremely graceful movements of the wrists and palms.**

Statement II is correct:**Nagabandha mudra, **in which the body is connected through curves in the shape of ‘8’,**is an **important posture in the Manipuri dance form. **Ras Leela (Radha-Krishna love story) is a recurring theme of the Manipuri dance recital.**

Statement III is incorrect: Mangalacharan and Tharijham are important elements of the Odissi dance (and not **Manipuri dance). **The drum – pung – is an intricate element of the recital in Manipuri dance.**Other Features of Manipuri Dance: **- While the** dance incorporates both Tandava and Lasya, **emphasis is laid on the latter. - The females wear unique long skirts. The focus is mainly on slow and gracious movements of hand and knee positions. - Flute, Khartals (wood clapper), dhols, etc. also accompany music. -**Compositions of Jayadeva and Chandidas are used extensively. **
- Thang-Ta and Sankirtana are also influenced by Manipuri dance.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
1. There are no parables in Upanishads.
2. Upanishads were composed earlier than the Puranas
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. There are no parables in Upanishads. 2. Upanishads were composed earlier than the Puranas Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Statement 1 is incorrect: **Upanishad literally means ‘approaching and sitting near’ and the texts contain conversations between teachers and students. Some Upanishads include parables.**For example, the wise beggar was a dialogue **based on a story from one of the most famous Upanishads, the Chhandogya Upanishad and antology of the two **birds was mentioned in the Katopanishad. **

Statement 2 is correct: Upanishads were composed as part of the later Vedic texts (1000 – 600 BCE) whereas the **Puranas were compiled in about 200 CE.**

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding Red Soils in India:
I. They develop on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern parts of the Deccan plateau.
II. They are rich in organic matter and nitrogen but deficient in iron compounds.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding Red Soils in India: I. They develop on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and southern parts of the Deccan plateau. II. They are rich in organic matter and nitrogen but deficient in iron compounds. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. Red soils are one of the major soil groups of India. They are mainly found in the Peninsular region. Their formation is closely related to the weathering of crystalline igneous and metamorphic rocks. Further, their agricultural productivity depends largely on irrigation and fertiliser use.**

Statement I is correct**.**Red soil develops on crystalline igneous rocks in areas of low rainfall in the eastern and **southern parts of the Deccan plateau. **It is found in parts of Tamil Nadu, Karnataka, north-east Andhra Pradesh, eastern parts of Madhya Pradesh, Bihar, West Bengal and Rajasthan.**

Statement II is incorrect**. Red soils contain**abundant iron compounds**. The iron gives the soil its**reddish**colour.**When hydrated, the soil appears yellow**. However, these soils are generally**poor in nitrogen, phosphorus and **humu**s. They are also**deficient in organic matter**.

statementLines: During the Delhi Sultanate period in India, the term ‘hashm-i qalb’ refers to:
questions.questionText: During the Delhi Sultanate period in India, the term ‘hashm-i qalb’ refers to:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. During the Delhi Sultanate period, the Sultans required the holders of iqtas, known as muqtis or walis, to maintain troops for royal service out of the taxes collected by them. However, certain areas were retained by the Sultans under their direct control, known as khalisa.**The forces stationed at Delhi were known as hashm-i qalb consisted of khasa **khel (royal slaves and guards) and afwaj-i qalb. Forces stationed at the provinces were called hashm-i atraf. **

questions.subject: Economy
statementLines: Which of the following statements with regard to the Minimum Support Price (MSP) in India is/are incorrect?
I. The MSP is announced by the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices based on the recommendation of the
Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA).
II. The MSP is announced annually for certain mandated agricultural crops.
III. While recommending MSP, factors such as cost of production, overall demand–supply situation of various crops in domestic and global markets, and inter-crop price parity are considered.
IV. The imputed value of the farmer’s family labour is not taken into account while calculating MSP.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to the Minimum Support Price (MSP) in India is/are incorrect? I. The MSP is announced by the Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices based on the recommendation of the Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (CCEA). II. The MSP is announced annually for certain mandated agricultural crops. III. While recommending MSP, factors such as cost of production, overall demand–supply situation of various crops in domestic and global markets, and inter-crop price parity are considered. IV. The imputed value of the farmer’s family labour is not taken into account while calculating MSP. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. Minimum Support Price is a policy instrument designed to provide income assurance to farmers. It acts as a safety net against sharp fall in market prices. The objective is not merely procurement, but stabilisation of farm income and encouragement of crop production in line with national needs.**

Statement I is incorrect**. The MSP is recommended by the**Commission for Agricultural Costs and Prices (CACP)**. The Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs (chaired by Prime Minister) approves the MSP before it is announced. The CCEA does not function as a recommending body; it is an approving authority of the Government.**

Statement II is correct**.**Annually, Government announces Minimum Support Prices (MSPs) for 22 mandated **agricultural crops **based on the recommendations of the Commission for Agricultural Costs & Prices (CACP), after considering the views of the concerned State Governments and Central Ministries/Departments.**

Statement III is correct**. While recommending MSP, CACP takes into account multiple economic factors. These include**cost of production**,**market price trends**,**domestic and international**demand–supply dynamics**, and the need to**maintain balance between different crops**.**Inter-crop price parity**ensures that farmers do not shift excessively towards one crop due to distorted price signals.**

Statement IV is incorrect**.**The imputed value of farmer’s family labour is included in cost calculations**. It forms part of the broader cost concept used while determining MSP and ignoring family labour would underestimate the true cost of cultivation.

statementLines: With reference to the Jute Corporation of India (JCI), which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Its mandate includes purchase of raw jute from farmers at the declared Minimum Support Price without a quantitative limit.
II. It serves as the price stabililising agency in the raw jute sector.
III. It is a Central Public Sector Enterprise under the administrative control of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers'
Welfare
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to the Jute Corporation of India (JCI), which of the following statements is/are correct? I. Its mandate includes purchase of raw jute from farmers at the declared Minimum Support Price without a quantitative limit. II. It serves as the price stabililising agency in the raw jute sector. III. It is a Central Public Sector Enterprise under the administrative control of the Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The Jute Corporation of India Limited was established in 1971, to protect the interests of jute growers and to stabilise the raw jute market. Jute cultivation is concentrated in eastern India, and price fluctuations can severely affect farmer income. One of its core responsibilities is to implement the**Minimum Support Price (MSP) operations for raw jute**whenever market prices fall below the announced support price. Through this mechanism, it prevents distress sales and stabilises the market**

Statement I is correct**. The**Jute Corporation Of India Limited**was incorporated as a price support agency with a clear mandate for the procurement of raw jute / mesta without any quantitative limit from the growers at the **minimum Support price (MSP)**declared in each year by the Government Of India based on the recommendations made by Commission for Agricultural Cost & Prices (CACP).**

Statement II is correct**. The Mission of Jute Corporation of India includes: 1. Implementation of the policy of the Government of India for providing Minimum Support Price (MSP) to the jute/mesta growers of the country.**2. **Serving as a price stabilising agency in the raw jute sector and taking the necessary measures in this respect. **3. Undertaking various extension measures for implementation of different jute related projects.**

Statement III is incorrect**. The**Jute Corporation Of India Limited is a Central Public Sector Enterprise under the **administrative control of the Ministry of Textiles**(not under** the **Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers' Welfare).

statementLines: Which of the following statements are correct with reference to the Vaisheshika School of Philosophy?
I. Kanada is regarded as the founder of this school.
II. It is the materialist school of Indian Philosophy.
III. It believes in God, as he is the authority of the Veda
IV. It believes that bondage is due to ignorance and liberation is attained through true knowledge.
Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are correct with reference to the Vaisheshika School of Philosophy? I. Kanada is regarded as the founder of this school. II. It is the materialist school of Indian Philosophy. III. It believes in God, as he is the authority of the Veda IV. It believes that bondage is due to ignorance and liberation is attained through true knowledge. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**. The Vaisheshika school is one of the six orthodox systems of Indian philosophy. It is known for its analytical and atomistic approach to reality. It attempts to explain the nature of the universe through categories of existence and logical reasoning.**

Statement I is correct**. The**Vaisheshika school was founded by the sage Kanada**. He composed the**Vaisheshika **Sutras**, which form the foundational text of this philosophical system.**

Statement II is incorrect**.**Cārvāka philosophy is the materialist school of Indian Philosophy. **The concept of reality of Cārvāka philosophy is based on a purely materialistic mechanism.**Vaiśeṣ**ika systems are known as the realistic schools of Indian philosophy. They are realistic, but not materialistic** (like Cārvāka philosophy), because they admit the existence of non-material substance like the soul as independent and separate.

**Statement III is correct**. T**he Vaisesika School believes in God as he is the authority of the Veda.** The Vaisheshika school strongly emphasises logical analysis and scientific categorisation of reality. According to Vaisheshika philosophy, the Vedas are authoritative because they are the word of God. God is described as the supreme soul. He is perfect, omniscient, omnipresent and eternal. He is the Lord who governs the universe. The school also accepts the law of karma. God operates in accordance with this law. He ensures that living beings receive the results of their past actions, whether merit or demerit.
**Statement IV is correct**. **The Vaisheshika school holds that bondage arises due to ignorance of the true nature of reality.** Liberation is achieved through right knowledge of categories such as substance, quality and action.
**Knowledge removes ignorance and leads to freedom from suffering**.**

questions.questionText: “The Brahmanas are a component of Hindu Shruti literature, attached to each Veda, and they primarily deal with the explanation of Vedic hymns, rituals, and sacrificial practices.” In this context, the Aitareya Brahmana is associated with which of the following Vedas?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The**Brahmanas**were composed to explain the rituals mentioned in the Vedas. They guide priests on how to perform sacrifices properly. They also interpret the symbolic meaning of sacred chants. Each Veda has its own Brahmana texts attached to it. The**Aitareya Brahmana**, associated with the**Rig Veda**and attributed to**Mahidasa Aitareya**, explains**Soma sacrifices**and other**Vedic rituals**, detailing priestly duties, procedures, and symbolic meanings. It also includes mythological narratives linking rituals to cosmic order (Rta) and references kingship rites like the**Rajasuya**, offering insights into early Vedic polity and society. Knowledge Base: The Brahmana texts associated with the four Vedas:**I. Rig Veda **a. Aitareya Brahmana b. Kaushitaki Brahmana**II. Sama Veda **c. Tandya Mahabrahmana d. Sadvimsha Brahmana**III. Yajur Veda **e. Taittiriya Brahmana f. Shatapatha Brahmana**IV. Atharva Veda ** II. Gopatha Brahmana

statementLines: Consider the following:

II. Vikramorvashi
III. Meghadutam
IV. Kumarasambhava
V. Abhigyan Shakuntalam
How many of the above works of literature were written by Kalidasa?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Priyadarsika II. Vikramorvashi III. Meghadutam IV. Kumarasambhava V. Abhigyan Shakuntalam How many of the above works of literature were written by Kalidasa?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer**.**Kalidasa**is regarded as one of the greatest Sanskrit poets and dramatists of ancient India. His works are known for their poetic beauty, refined language and deep understanding of human emotions.** Option I is incorrect**.**Priyadarsika**was written by**King Harsha (Harshavardhana**). It is a Sanskrit drama that revolves around romance and royal life.  Option II is correct**.**Vikramorvashi (also called Vikramorvasiyam**) is a Sanskrit drama written by**Kalidasa**. It tells the love story of King Pururavas and the celestial nymph Urvashi. The play blends mythology with human emotions.** Option III is correct**.**Meghadutam**is a lyrical poem written by**Kalidasa**. It is about a Yaksha who sends a message to his wife through a passing cloud. The poem is famous for its vivid description of landscapes and deep emotional expression of separation.** Option IV is correct**.**Kumarasambhava**is an epic written by**Kalidasa**. This is a unique epic containing the birth description of Devasenapati Kumaraskandha along with the love description of Shiva Parvati.** Option V is correct**.**Abhigyan Shakuntalam**is one of**Kalidasa’s**most celebrated plays. It narrates the story of King Dushyanta and Shakuntala. The theme revolves around love, separation and reunion. It gained worldwide fame and was later translated into many languages.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. India has a rich tradition of religious literature belonging to different faiths such as Hinduism, Buddhism and Jainism.** Option I is incorrect**.**Niyamasara is a Jain philosophical text**. It was composed by Acharya Kundakunda, an important Digambara Jain monk. The text focuses on the nature of the soul and the path to liberation. It emphasises right conduct and inner discipline.** Option II is incorrect**.**Uvasaggaharam Stotra is a revered Jain devotional hymn**. It is traditionally attributed to Acharya Bhadrabahu. The hymn is dedicated to Lord Parshvanatha, the 23rd Tirthankara of Jainism. It is recited by Jain devotees for spiritual protection and removal of obstacles.** Option III is incorrect**.**Kalakacharya Katha is a Jain narrative text**. It recounts the story of the Jain monk Kalakacharya and his interaction with the Saka rulers. The narrative reflects Jain historical traditions and religious values.

statementLines: With reference to medieval Indo-Persian literature, the Razmnāma was a Persian translation of the Sanskrit epic

questions.questionText: With reference to medieval Indo-Persian literature, the Razmnāma was a Persian translation of the Sanskrit epic Mahābhārata, patronised by which among the following Mughal Emperors?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**.**Razmnāma (The Book of War), was one of the several literary gems patronized by Akbar in the 1580s.**The **Razmnāma was a Persian translation of the Sanskrit epic Mahābhārata. **The etymology of the Persian word Razmnāma implied Tale or Book of War and is read as “Razm” implying “war” and “nama” implying “book”, “history” or “epic”. Akbar encouraged the translation of important Sanskrit texts into Persian so that they could be understood by the Persian-speaking elite of his court. The translation work was undertaken by scholars under royal supervision.**Badauni**was one of the scholars involved in this project. Through such efforts, Akbar promoted cultural exchange between Hindu and Muslim intellectual traditions.

questions.subject: History
statementLines: The irrigation device called ‘Araghatta’ was
questions.questionText: The irrigation device called ‘Araghatta’ was

options.a: A water bag made of leather pulled over a pulley.
options.b: A large wheel with earthen pots tied to the outer ends of its spokes.
options.c: A larger earthen pot driven by bullocks.
options.d: A large water bucket pulled up by rope directly by hand.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **

Option b is correct: **In India,**the irrigation device called araghatta or arahatta was one wheel with pitchers or pots **of clay attached around the rim of the wheel. **It was also called ‘Ghanti-Yantra’. Araghatta is a Persian wheel,**a **mechanical water-lifting device, used in medieval India to lift water from wells for irrigation. **- The**essential part of the araghatta was the ghati-yantra or the device with pitchers, usually mounted on **the wheel, **but not attached to its rim. The ghati-yantra as an irrigation device is therefore often held as a pot garland. - The**araghatta seems to have drawn water from a reservoir which in turn received its water from an **irrigation well. **The spokes (arakas) of a wheel was a revolving apparatus though it does not refer to any gearing mechanism enabling the conversion of the horizontal rotary motion into a vertical rotary motion. The latter feature which became visible from the fourteenth century onwards, represented the typical Persian wheel or the saqia.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Row I is correct: Kathputli: String puppetry: Rajasthan **Kathputli is the** traditional string puppet theatre of Rajasthan. **The word Kathputli literally means “wooden doll” (kath = wood, putli = doll). The puppets are controlled by strings attached to their limbs and are operated from above. The dolls do not have legs and the long skirt covers the lower portion. Row II is correct: Togalu Gombeyatta: Shadow puppetry: Karnataka **Togalu Gombeyatta is a traditional**shadow puppet theatre of Karnataka**. “**Togalu” means leather**, and the puppets are made of treated leather and cast shadows on a screen with the help of light.**Row III is correct: Tholapavakoothu/Pavakoothu: Glove puppetry: Kerala **Pavakoothu is a**glove puppetry form of Kerala**and is also most popular glove puppetry in India. It is**influenced by **the Kathakali**(classical dance from Kerela). The themes Pavakoothu are generally taken from the episodes of**Ramayana **and**Mahabharata. **Row IV is correct: Putul Nachh: Rod puppetry: West Bengal **Putul Nachh is a rod puppetry form of West Bengal. **The puppets are attached to long rods, which are used to control their movements. Since**all four pairs are correctly matched, **the** correct answer is d) All the four**.

statementLines: With reference to the administrative divisions of the Vijayanagara Empire,
which one of the following is the correct sequence in ascending order in terms of size?
questions.questionText: With reference to the administrative divisions of the Vijayanagara Empire, which one of the following is the correct sequence in ascending order in terms of size?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Vijayanagara rulers (AD 1336–1672**) developed a highly structured administrative system to manage their vast territories in Southern India. The**empire was divided into different administrative units **to ensure efficient governance:**1. Mandalam (Province):**This was the**largest administrative unit**, equivalent to a modern-day state. Each Mandalam was usually placed under the charge of a governor, known as a Mandaleshvara or Nayak.**2. Nadu (District**): Each**Mandalam was further divided into Nadus**. These functioned as the primary units of local administration and revenue.**3. Sthala (Sub-district):**The Nadu was subdivided into Sthalas. This unit typically comprised a group of several villages.**4. Grama (Village**): The**Grama was the basic, smallest unit of administration**. Hence, the**correct sequence in ascending order (smallest to largest) for the administrative divisions of the **Vijayanagara Empire is a) Sthala – Nadu – Mandalam**.

statementLines: With reference to the Sahitya Akademi, consider the following statements:
I. It is a statutory body.
II. It functions as India’s National Academy of Letters to promote literary activities in 24 Indian languages, including
English.
III. One of its key functions is to confer the Jnanpith Award annually for outstanding contributions to Indian literature across languages.
IV. It is an autonomous organization under the Ministry of Culture, Government of India.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Sahitya Akademi, consider the following statements: I. It is a statutory body. II. It functions as India’s National Academy of Letters to promote literary activities in 24 Indian languages, including English. III. One of its key functions is to confer the Jnanpith Award annually for outstanding contributions to Indian literature across languages. IV. It is an autonomous organization under the Ministry of Culture, Government of India. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect: Sahitya Akademi is not a statutory body**. It**was established in 1954**by the**Government of **India Resolution**as an**autonomous organization**and is registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860. There is no separate Act of Parliament creating it.**

Statement II is correct**: Sahitya Akademi is officially**recognized as India's National Academy of Letters**. It**promotes **literature**, literary dialogue, publications, translations, and cultural unity through activities in**24 recognized Indian **languages**(including the**22 Eighth Schedule languages of the Indian Constitution plus English and Rajasthani). **

Statement III is incorrect:**The**Jnanpith Award is conferred by Bharatiya Jnanpith**(a private cultural trust),**not by **Sahitya Akademi**.**Sahitya Akademi confers its own awards, including the Sahitya Akademi Award**(for outstanding books in**24 languages**), Yuva Puraskar, Bal Sahitya Puraskar, and Translation Prize. The**Jnanpith is India's highest literary honour**but is independent of the Akademi.**

Statement IV is correct:**Sahitya Akademi is an**autonomous body**under**the the**Ministry of Culture**, Government of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**UNESCO World Heritage List includes cultural and natural properties**that possess Outstanding Universal Value (OUV) and meet at least one of UNESCOs selection criteria for global significance and protection. I. These sites represent humanitys shared heritage and are recognised for their historical, architectural, artistic, or cultural importance.**II. India has 44 World Heritage Sites**(as of 2026), comprising**thirty-six cultural, seven natural and one mixed site,**reflecting the countrys diverse civilisational legacy and heritage traditions** Option I is correct: Maratha Military Landscapes of India **a)**It comprises 12 forts across Maharashtra**including Raigad, Shivneri, and Sindhudurg. b) This network played a key role in the Marathas rise as a major political and military force.**c) **It was**inscribed as a UNESCO Cultural World Heritage Site recently (2025). ** Option II is incorrect: River Island of Majuli in the midstream of the Brahamaputra River in Assam **1.**Majuli in Assam is the largest river island in the world, **with a rich Vaishnavite cultural tradition.**2. **It is on the UNESCO Tentative List, **recognized for its unique cultural practices and satras (monastic institutions), but not formally inscribed as a World Heritage Site. ** Option III is correct: Sacred Ensembles of the Hoysalas **It**includes temples at Belur, Halebidu, and Somanathapura**in**Karnataka**, which represents the three most representative examples of**Hoysala-style temple complexes**in southern India, dating from the**12th to 13th **centuries**. It was**inscribed in 2023 as a UNESCO Cultural World Heritage Site. ** Option IV is incorrect: Sun Temple, Modhera and its adjoining monuments **Located in**Gujara**t, this temple is famous for its chariot-shaped design, intricate carvings, and astronomical alignment. It is** currently on the UNESCO Tentative List, **but**unlike the Konark Sun Temple in Odisha, **it has** not been **inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List. ** Option V is correct: Moidams-the Mound-Burial System of the Ahom Dynasty **These are**burial mounds of Ahom kings in Assam**, often called the**Pyramids of Assam**. Recognized for their historical and cultural significance in preserving Ahom heritage.
